article.title: RSAC 2026 Crowns an AI Agent Security Startup as the Industry Converges on Its Most Urgent Unsolved Problem
article.summary: Geordie AI wins the Innovation Sandbox as nearly every major cybersecurity vendor at the 35th annual RSA Conference unveils products to secure autonomous AI agents.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The 35th annual RSA Conference, held March 23–26 at San Francisco's Moscone Center, drew cybersecurity professionals from more than 100 countries for over 570 sessions across 31 tracks, according to [the conference organizers](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/35th-annual-rsac-conference-opens-flagship-event-in-san-francisco-on-monday-302721594.html). One theme overshadowed all others: how to secure the autonomous AI agents now proliferating across enterprise environments.

The conference's marquee startup competition, the Innovation Sandbox, crystallized the trend. Geordie AI, a company building a security and governance platform purpose-built for AI agents, was named Most Innovative Startup, beating nine other finalists for the title and a $5 million investment, as [announced by the RSAC organizers](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/geordie-ai-named-most-innovative-startup-at-rsac-2026-conference-innovation-sandbox-contest-302722519.html).

## The Innovation Sandbox

The Innovation Sandbox contest has run since 2005, and its alumni include some of the industry's most consequential companies. Past finalists have collectively achieved more than 100 acquisitions and attracted over $50.1 billion in investment, a track record highlighted by Google's $32 billion acquisition of 2021 finalist Wiz—a deal that was formally completed just days before this year's conference, according to [RSAC's finalists announcement](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/finalists-announced-for-rsac-innovation-sandbox-contest-2026-302683184.html).

This year's ten finalists each received a $5 million investment through the SAFE program. Beyond winner Geordie AI, the cohort included Charm Security (AI-powered social engineering prevention), Clearly AI (automated security reviews in software development), Crash Override (CI/CD provenance and SLSA compliance), Fig Security (security operations resilience), Glide Identity (passwordless authentication), Humanix (conversational AI for social engineering detection), Realm Labs (AI model inference visibility), Token Security (non-human identity governance), and ZeroPath (AI-native code security), according to [the finalists announcement](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/finalists-announced-for-rsac-innovation-sandbox-contest-2026-302683184.html). Of the ten finalists, at least four—Geordie AI, Realm Labs, Token Security, and ZeroPath—are focused specifically on securing AI systems or the identities they create.

Henry Comfort, co-founder and CEO of Geordie AI, said in the [winner announcement](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/geordie-ai-named-most-innovative-startup-at-rsac-2026-conference-innovation-sandbox-contest-302722519.html) that the recognition was "great validation for the problem we are solving, the team behind it all putting it together, and the customers we're now solving problems for."

## An Industry Pivots to Agent Security

The startup contest mirrored announcements from virtually every major cybersecurity vendor. CrowdStrike introduced Falcon Data Security and EDR AI Runtime Protection, extending its platform to detect shadow AI deployments and monitor agent behavior at the endpoint level, according to [BusinessWire](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260323453078/en/CrowdStrike-Introduces-Falcon-Data-Security-to-Stop-Data-Theft-Across-the-Agentic-Enterprise).

Cisco unveiled DefenseClaw, an open-source tool that scans MCP servers, plugins, and other resources AI agents rely on for security vulnerabilities, with installation taking roughly five minutes, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/23/cisco-debuts-new-ai-agent-security-features-open-source-defenseclaw-tool/). The company also expanded its Duo identity platform with features for registering AI agents, tracking which employees use them, and defining granular tool-access rules. DJ Sampath, senior vice president of Cisco's AI and software platform business, told [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/23/cisco-debuts-new-ai-agent-security-features-open-source-defenseclaw-tool/) that when a skill is blocked, "its sandbox permissions are revoked, its files are quarantined, and the agent gets an error if it tries to invoke it."

The volume of announcements was striking. As ZK Research founder Zeus Kerravala observed in a panel covered by [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/26/rsac-2026-spotlights-data-security-chaos-ai-age-rsac26/), securing AI agents is "unlike anything the security industry's ever had to deal with before," in part because agents that spin up, spawn child agents, and deprecate themselves break every assumption underpinning traditional identity and access management.

## What We Don't Know

The scale of vendor activity at RSAC 2026 makes the urgency clear, but several questions remain unanswered.

No consensus has emerged on how to establish behavioral baselines for autonomous agents, making anomaly detection fundamentally harder than it is for human users. As theCUBE Research analyst Christophe Bertrand noted in the [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/26/rsac-2026-spotlights-data-security-chaos-ai-age-rsac26/) panel, "We're sort of building the plane as we're flying it."

It is also unclear whether the current wave of products will consolidate around platform plays from incumbents like CrowdStrike and Palo Alto Networks, or whether purpose-built startups like Geordie AI will carve out durable market positions—a pattern that has played out repeatedly in previous cybersecurity cycles.

Finally, while vendors presented solutions for discovering and governing AI agents within enterprises, few addressed the harder problem of securing inter-organization agent-to-agent communication, a scenario that will grow as agentic workflows cross corporate boundaries.

## Analysis

RSAC has long served as a barometer for where the cybersecurity industry believes its next existential challenge lies. In 2026, the answer is unambiguous: the autonomous AI agent. The fact that the Innovation Sandbox winner, four of its ten finalists, and nearly every major platform vendor converged on the same problem in the same week suggests that agent security is entering the rapid-investment phase that cloud security experienced a decade ago. Whether that investment translates into effective protection before threat actors exploit the same agentic capabilities offensively remains the defining open question.
